segments.text: This is my trading journal from 2014. I lost $35,000 that year and if I'm being
honest I deserve to lose every penny of it. Not because I wasn't smart but because
I was arrogant about things I didn't understand. Today I'm gonna give you the
seven lessons. I wish someone had beaten into my head before I started but
lesson number five that's the one that almost made me quit and it's probably
the one you're ignoring right now.
Before we get into the seven lessons I need to set the table here. This isn't
gonna be a highlight reel. I'm not gonna tell you about my wins. I'm gonna show
you exactly where I fail. The mistakes I made, the money I lost and the
lessons I paid for because here's the thing. Every successful trader has a
story like this. They just don't talk about it. So today I'm talking about it.
Not to discourage you but to save you the years of pain I went through
learning these the expensive way. Alright let's go. So here's what nobody tells
beginners. The skills that make you successful in your normal life. You know
confidence, persistence, strong opinions, taking action. Those same skills will
destroy you in trading. Trading rewards the opposite. Confidence becomes
healthy skepticism in trading. Persistence becomes cutting losses fast. Strong
opinions become flexibility. Taking action becomes waiting. You have to redefine
what success actually looks like. You have to unlearn the perception of success
and relearn trading success. So every one of these seven lessons it's not new
information to learn. It's actually old programming to delete and that's way
harder especially lesson five because lesson five costs me more than money. It
almost cost me my entire trading career. Let me show you where most people start
going wrong. Lesson number one. Paper trading is completely lying to you and
it's you lying to yourself. I spent six months paper trading. I built up my
confidence through paper trading. 83% win rate. I felt completely invincible. I
went live with real money. 31% win rate. Why? Because paper trading doesn't
activate your fear response at all. It does it a little bit but it's not real.
You can't simulate the feeling of watching $2,000 real dollars evaporate. Less than
90 seconds. You just can't. The better approach. Once you have a playbook,
once you've learned a trade start with real money. Start small with your sizing.
50 shares, 100 shares, even one share. Just enough that losing hurts a little
because that's where the real learning happens. Lesson number two. More screen
time does not equal better results. I was like going gung ho. I was watching
charts like it felt like 12 hours a day. I thought I was putting in the work. I
thought I was grinding. My coach sat me down and said you're not learning
your spectating. I cut it down to five focused hours of chart work a day. Five
focused hours of trading work a day. I stopped spectating and I started playing.
My win rate went up 14% in two weeks because I was only trading when I was
focused. I wasn't spectating and then trying to jump in when I was exhausted and
making tons of mistakes. I focused on quality over quantity. You should focus on
quality over quantity always. Lesson number three. Your first strategy will fail
and that's really good. Most traders think failure means they pick the wrong
strategy. So they abandoned it and they move to the next one. Wrong. Your first
strategy fails because you're learning execution. The strategy says a scope for
you to learn execution. Not because the strategy's bad. Not because the trades
bad or it's not for you. The strategy's just the vehicle to learn position
sizing to learn risk management to learn emotional control and most importantly
to learn exit discipline. Once you learn those the strategy starts working. So
when your first strategy fails don't run. Focus on your execution. Fix your
execution. Lesson number four. Position sizing matters ten times more than
your entry does. You can have the perfect set up. You can have the perfect
trade. You can have the perfect entry. You can have the perfect read but if you
size it wrong none of that matters. I watched two traders take the exact same
setup like two days ago. One made four thousand dollars. The other wound up
losing twenty two hundred. Same entry. Different outcomes. One size that
correctly from the start. He didn't oversize. He didn't undersize. He sized it
for what his bet sizing calculator said it should be. He followed his plan and
he simply took the profits when he had the opportunity to take profits. The
other one started small. Then when the trade worked he added late to try and make
more. He had started so small that when it started to work and it was really
clean this trigger happened in his brain and he was like well if I can just add to
it then I'll really make a ton on this trade. I'll make what I wanted to on this
trade by starting small. He put himself in a bad position because guess what
happened almost as soon as he added the stock reversed immediately and he turned
a winning trade into a loser all because of where and how he sized your edge
isn't just the setup. It's the setup plus the right size for your emotional
infrastructure. You get that wrong and your analysis doesn't matter at all. Lesson
number five this one's a key. You're going to lose money your first year budget
for it. Five months into trading I was down $31,000. I remember sitting at my
desk at like two in the morning looking at my account looking at my journals
thinking what's wrong with me. I'd read the books. I put in hundreds of hours. I
backed test strategies and I was failing. My friend asked me how long did it take
you to get good at lacrosse right? I played in high school like I said thought
about it and I was like a year before I wasn't embarrassing myself and then
three total before I was even competitive right and he kind of laughed and he
goes but you thought you'd be a really profitable trader in like five months
and I didn't have an answer. That's when it hit me. I wasn't treating trading
like a skill to develop. I was treating it like a test to pass and the market
doesn't give grades it just gives feedback. Markets are opportunity generating
machines but even more importantly their feedback generating machines and I
wasn't listening to any of the feedback. I was just looking at the opportunity
and saying why can't I get more of that? Who's giving me feedback as to why I
couldn't? I wasn't listening. Losing $31,000 wasn't failure. It was tuition but I
didn't have that much left in savings so I made a rule. I didn't have that much
runway so I made a rule. Alright 18 months at $1,000 per month max lost. That gave
me a runway. I said a really clear time oriented goal and I gave myself a
clear budget of how much I had per month. That was it period. If I couldn't
figure it out in the next 18 months I was gonna walk away. That budget changed
everything. It forced me to slow down. It forced me to actually learn instead of
trying to win. It forced me to take that feedback instead of just chasing the
next opportunity. It took me almost 13 to those 18 months to really turn the
corner and generate consistent trading profits. If I am budgeted for that
learning curve I probably would have quit a month eight. I probably wouldn't have
had the runway to even make it to month eight. So here's what I tell every
new trader now. You need 12 to 18 months of runway. Not to make it but to learn
to accept that feedback and learn shortens the learning curve. That's all it
does. You have to budget for it. Don't expect to be perfect right at the
beginning. Budget for it. If you can't afford 12 months of tuition you really
can't afford to trade full-time yet. What you need to do is focus on one set up
one opportunity, one trade and you need to let that fall into your lap and you
need to learn how to take the feedback generated by the market and start
incorporating that because that's how you move forward. Let's talk about number
six because your trading style will find you when you're listening to the
feedback. For a while when I first joined the desk I tried to trade like the guy
sitting next to me. He was crushing it with these gap trades right. My first
three months I lost for three months straight on those gap trades. It just wasn't
my style. I'd get the trade I'd get exactly what I was thinking about and then
the next little progression would start to occur to me and so I'd make the
mistake we talked about earlier. I'd size it wrong. I'd put together the
wrong expectations for the trade. I couldn't hold when it went against me on a
weird gap. I didn't understand it. It really wasn't my trade. I need to feel
the flow. I need to understand the pattern. I need to understand why that
pattern's developing. I needed to wait for my edge. Your personality dictates your
style, not the other way around. So stop trying to force someone else's
approach into your trading. Let's talk about lesson number seven here. Your
trading community who you surround yourself with who you talk to matters more
than you think. I tried to do it alone initially for like four or five months. I
thought I could figure it out myself. I thought I could just be and somebody
looking in from the outside. Take all the pieces, put them together in this
beautiful mixture of everything and then figure it out right. I was wrong. The
moment I set a budget, the next thing I did was realize I needed to reach out
to other traders to fit and get the feedback I needed every single day and every
single day, everything started accelerating. Not because they gave me secrets, but
in part because talking to other traders normalized the struggle that I was
going through. I realized even the traders I admired were fighting their own
battles. That's when I understood the traders who make it are the ones who stack
small wins day after day, month after month, and ultimately year after year.
Trading alone is brutal. Don't do it if you don't have to. Here's one bonus
lesson because when I was walking in here, it just occurred to me based on
an experience we had today that consistency beats heroics every single time.
I mean, thinking back to my best month from that year when I started, I think it
was plus like 47,000, right? My worst month, I was probably minus 39 or 40 or 42,
something like that. Both of them happened when I was taking big swings and what
was worse was I started to feel invincible. I was taking these big swings and
positions and I wasn't normalizing anything. Those big swings kind of shut off
my ability to take the feedback generated by the market and I just started
focusing on how do I make more? You know, all of the things that you need to not do.
And now the goal and what we what inspired this last lesson today was targeting
consistency, boring, repeatable, compounding consistency because that's how
wealth actually gets built. Not with hero trades, but with consistent execution.
This is a skill development game period. Focus on building the skill. All right,
let's summarize all this. Lesson one, paper trading lies. Start small with real
money so you can experience the feelings of real capital. Number two, screen time
doesn't equal results. Focus on quality over quantity. Number three, your first
strategy will fail. So go ahead and fix the execution before abandoning the
strategy. Number four, position sizing matters more than your entry. Number five,
budget for losses. Expect a 12 to 18 month learning curve if you need it, but
certainly budget each month for how long your runway is. Number six, your style is
going to find you. Listen to the feedback generated by the market. Seven, community
matters. Reach out to other traders, normalize the things that you're going
through. And number eight, our bonus lesson, consistency beats heroics. These
aren't tips. These are survival rules. I paid $35,000 to learn them. You just
got them from free. But free information is kind of worthless unless you actually
use it. So here's how you can use it. Pick three of these lessons. Write them on a
sticky note. Put it up right on your monitor. Not all the lessons, just three. Which
three are you going to choose? Look, you're going to lose money learning this. That's
not pessimism. It's just the truth. But you can lose $35,000 like I did almost
chaotically learning the same lessons over and over not listening to the feedback. Or
you can lose $3,000 or $5,000 or even less strategically with a budget, a timeline,
and a plan. The market really doesn't care which one you choose, but your future does.
You can't skip the tuition, but you can actually make it count. So let's comment below
and tell me which of these lessons hit you the hardest. And if you're just starting
up, tell me what your learning budget and your timeline is. And we'll actually tell
you if it's realistic. This isn't about scaring you. It's about preparing you because the
traders who make it aren't the most talented. They're the ones who respected the learning
curve. They develop the skills needed to survive. And then the consistency needed to accelerate.
Stop watching charts all day hoping for trades. Our Wall Street scanner alerts you minutes
before a profitable setup triggers, just like it does for a $20 million a year traders.
At S&B Capital, we're not your typical secretive Wall Street firm. Instead of hiding our strategies,
we built our success by sharing them. Our New York Trading Floor houses dozens of professional
traders, many of whom are seven figure and even eight figure per year earners. And we hire
select remote traders worldwide. Are you constantly feeling like becoming consistently profitable
is just around the corner, but you're never actually getting there. Here's what we've learned
in nearly 20 years of training elite traders. Scalping is by far the fastest and easiest way to become
consistently profitable in US stocks. Now we're talking about hit and run trading in and out of the
market within five to 30 minutes. Here's the challenge though. When you're trading alone without the
support of a proper firm environment, it's extremely difficult to find good scalps consistently.
The key is being in the right stocks at the right time and spotting what we call easy money setups.
How much time and money have you lost trading without real world edge? That's exactly why we developed
the SMB scalp radar, a groundbreaking tool that does 80% of the heavy lifting by scanning US stocks
in real time. So we've programmed it with five of our most powerful scalping strategies,
the same ones that have collectively made our firm tens of millions of dollars.
The radar identifies the 20 to 40 stocks in play during each session. It detects 7 to 12 high
quality scalping opportunities daily. It alerts you minutes before these setups trigger,
giving you time to prepare. And then it monitors the market in real time so you never actually miss a
setup. Look, there's a reason why professional trading floors work. Traders helping traders spot
and execute their best setups in real time. And that's exactly what the scalp radar does for
independent traders like you. It's having a team of professional traders scanning the market
alongside you, pointing out the highest probability setups even before they happen. What would it
mean to have the same advantages as professional traders on a Wall Street trading floor? And because
these are the exact same setups, our professional traders use every day. You're not just getting
some theoretical system. You're actually getting battle tested strategies that actually work in today's
